evidence_quotes.quote: Adobe Creative Cloud, which locks users out of their software if the program cannot verify their subscription online at least once every 30 or 99 days.
Call of Duty: Black Ops 6 (2024) require an online connection even for its single-player campaign, meaning it cannot be used at all without internet access.

Overwatch (2016), a fully-priced game, was shut down in 2022 and replaced by Overwatch 2. Players lost access to the original game, regardless of purchase, and were forced into a different experience.
Knockout City, a live-service multiplayer game, ended its official servers in 2023.
SimCity (2013) required online connectivity even for single-player modes at launch, resulting in a broken experience when servers failed.

paragraphs.text: Hello! As two of the points listed under section B. Objectives and policy options refer specifically to issues related to the sale and use of digital products and services, I feel it would be highly relevant for the Digital Fairness Act to include explicit protections ensuring that consumers retain the right to continue using digital products they have purchased. This includes the right to be able to use local software and applications without being required to maintain an internet connection, and the right to retain access even after the seller discontinues support or online services. A major concern is the growing trend of requiring an internet connection for software and applications where the core functionality does not rely on being online. This practice unnecessarily limits consumer access and creates artificial dependency on a seller's continued operation. When those services eventually shut down, users can lose access entirely to tools and content they already paid for. Some examples of software and applications that are currently impacted include: Adobe Creative Cloud, which locks users out of their software if the program cannot verify their subscription online at least once every 30 or 99 days. This can affect users during travel, in rural areas, or when the servers eventually shut down. Avid Pro Tools, a professional audio workstation, also requires regular online license checks, even for perpetual license holders. Users can lose access to essential tools simply because of a server error or missed validation. Microsoft Office 365 and Autodesk software depend on cloud-based licensing that can cause downtime or even total loss of access when servers are unavailable. In gaming, the same problem is often even more severe: Call of Duty: Black Ops 6 (2024) require an online connection even for its single-player campaign, meaning it cannot be used at all without internet access. Overwatch (2016), a fully-priced game, was shut down in 2022 and replaced by Overwatch 2. Players lost access to the original game, regardless of purchase, and were forced into a different experience. Knockout City, a live-service multiplayer game, ended its official servers in 2023. However, the developers released a private server version for players to host themselves, showing that more consumer-friendly EOL options are possible. SimCity (2013) required online connectivity even for single-player modes at launch, resulting in a broken experience when servers failed. An offline mode was added only after heavy backlash. Consumers are not asking for lifetime support or feature updates, but simply the ability to continue using what they already own. These online restrictions are rarely technical necessities and instead function more as control mechanisms that benefit the seller at the expense of the buyer. To address this, the Digital Fairness Act should: Prohibit requiring an internet connection for the basic operation of software or applications that can reasonably function offline Require clear and effective end-of-life (EOL) plans for software that does rely on online components, including access to offline fallback versions, server-independent modes, the ability for users to host their own servers once official support ends or other means of continuing use once support ends This would help reduce unnecessary obsolescence and protect the long-term value of digital purchases. Consumers should not be forced to lose access to paid software simply because a server goes down, a login fails or a company decides to sunset a service. By including protections for the continued usability of purchased digital products, the Digital Fairness Act would take a crucial step toward ensuring long-term consumer rights in the digital market.
